Description: Use sql.Open to create a connection pool and db.PingContext to verify connectivity before serving traffic.
Do: Always call db.PingContext after sql.Open to verify the database is reachable.
Dont: Do not assume sql.Open validates the connection; it only initializes the pool.

Description: Configure connection pool limits to prevent exhausting database connections and to reuse idle connections efficiently.
Do: Set MaxOpenConns and MaxIdleConns based on your workload and database limits.
Dont: Do not leave defaults unbounded; unlimited open connections can overwhelm the database.
Code Good:
db.SetMaxOpenConns(25)
db.SetMaxIdleConns(10)
db.SetConnMaxLifetime(5 * time.Minute)
db.SetConnMaxIdleTime(1 * time.Minute)

Description: Use context-aware query methods to support cancellation and timeout propagation from upstream callers.
Do: Use QueryContext and ExecContext instead of Query and Exec.
Dont: Do not use non-context query methods; they cannot be cancelled or timed out.
Code Bad:
rows, err := db.Query("SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE active = $1", true)
// No context, no timeout, no cancellation support

Description: Use errors.Is(err, sql.ErrNoRows) to distinguish a missing row from real errors when using QueryRow.
Do: Check for sql.ErrNoRows explicitly when a missing row is a valid business case.
Dont: Do not treat sql.ErrNoRows as a fatal error when absence is expected.

Description: Wrap multi-statement operations in a transaction and always defer a rollback so it runs on error paths.
Do: Defer tx.Rollback immediately after Begin; it is a no-op after Commit.
Dont: Do not forget to rollback on error; leaked transactions hold locks and connections.
Code Bad:
tx, _ := db.BeginTx(ctx, nil)
tx.ExecContext(ctx, "UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - $1 WHERE id = $2", amount, from)
tx.ExecContext(ctx, "UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + $1 WHERE id = $2", amount, to)
tx.Commit()
// No rollback on error, no error handling

Description: Prepare statements once and reuse them to avoid repeated parse and plan overhead on the database server.
Do: Prepare statements at initialization time and reuse the *sql.Stmt across calls.
Dont: Do not prepare statements inside hot loops; it causes repeated parsing on every call.

Description: Use sql.Null types to safely scan nullable columns without panicking on NULL values.
Do: Use sql.NullString or sql.NullInt64 for columns that can be NULL.
Dont: Do not scan nullable columns into plain Go types; NULL will cause a scan error.

Description: Define GORM models with proper struct tags and embed gorm.Model for ID and timestamp fields.
Do: Embed gorm.Model and use struct tags for column constraints and indexes.
Dont: Do not manually define ID and timestamp fields when gorm.Model provides them.

Description: Use GORM hooks to inject logic before or after database operations without cluttering business code.
Do: Use hooks for cross-cutting concerns like UUID generation or audit logging.
Dont: Do not put hook-like logic in every caller; centralize it in the model hook.

Description: Define scopes as functions that return func(*gorm.DB) *gorm.DB to compose reusable query conditions.
Do: Define scopes for common filters and chain them with db.Scopes.
Dont: Do not duplicate WHERE clauses across multiple query sites.

Description: Use db.Transaction for automatic commit on nil error and rollback on non-nil error.
Do: Use the db.Transaction callback which handles commit and rollback automatically.
Dont: Do not manually Begin/Commit/Rollback with GORM unless you need savepoints.
Code Bad:
tx := db.Begin()
tx.Create(&order)
tx.Create(&payment)
tx.Commit()
// No error check, no rollback on failure

Description: Use Preload for eager loading associations and Joins for inner-join based loading.
Do: Use Preload for has-many associations and Joins for belongs-to to reduce queries.
Dont: Do not load associations in a loop causing N+1 queries.
Code Good:
var users []User
db.Preload("Orders").Preload("Orders.Items").Find(&users)
// Or use Joins for belongs-to
var orders []Order
db.Joins("User").Find(&orders)

Description: Use sqlx named queries with :param syntax for readability and to bind struct fields automatically.
Do: Use NamedExecContext with structs for insert and update operations.
Dont: Do not manually order positional parameters for large INSERT statements.
Code Bad:
_, err := db.ExecContext(ctx, "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ($1, $2)", u.Name, u.Email)
// With many columns, positional args become error-prone

Description: Use sqlx.Get for single rows and sqlx.Select for multiple rows to scan directly into structs.
Do: Use db tag on struct fields and call Get or Select for automatic scanning.
Dont: Do not manually scan each column when sqlx can map them for you.

Description: Use sqlx.In to expand slice arguments into SQL IN clauses safely.
Do: Use sqlx.In then Rebind the query for the target database driver.
Dont: Do not build IN clauses by concatenating strings; it risks SQL injection.

Description: Configure sqlc.yaml to define database engine and code generation settings.
Do: Create a sqlc.yaml with version 2 schema specifying engine and query paths.
Dont: Do not skip the configuration file; sqlc requires it to generate correct code.
Code Good:
# sqlc.yaml
version: "2"
sql:
  - engine: "postgresql"
    queries: "query/"
    schema: "schema/"
    gen:
      go:
        package: "db"
        out: "internal/db"
        sql_package: "pgx/v5"
        emit_json_tags: true
Code Bad:
# No sqlc.yaml
# Running sqlc generate without config
# Results in: error: no sqlc.yaml found

Description: Write annotated SQL with -- name: and command type so sqlc generates type-safe Go functions.
Do: Annotate every query with -- name: FuncName :command for proper generation.
Dont: Do not write unannotated SQL files; sqlc will skip them.

Description: Let sqlc generate Go structs and query functions; do not modify generated code manually.
Do: Use the generated Queries struct and call generated methods with proper params.
Dont: Do not modify generated files; your changes will be overwritten on next sqlc generate.

Description: Write paired up and down migration files so migrations can be applied and rolled back cleanly.
Do: Create numbered migration files with both up and down SQL.
Dont: Do not write migrations without a corresponding down file; rollback becomes impossible.
Code Bad:
-- 000001_create_users.up.sql
CREATE TABLE users (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL
);
-- No down migration file exists
-- Cannot rollback this migration

Description: Use golang-migrate to apply versioned migrations via the CLI or embed them in your Go application.
Do: Use the Go API with embed.FS to bundle and apply migrations at startup.
Dont: Do not run raw SQL files manually; use a migration tool for versioning and tracking.

Description: Use multi-value INSERT or COPY for bulk inserts instead of inserting rows one at a time in a loop.
Do: Build a single INSERT with multiple VALUE tuples or use COPY protocol.
Dont: Do not insert rows one by one in a loop; it is extremely slow for large datasets.
Code Good:
valueStrings := make([]string, 0, len(users))
valueArgs := make([]interface{}, 0, len(users)*2)
for i, u := range users {
    valueStrings = append(valueStrings, fmt.Sprintf("($%d, $%d)", i*2+1, i*2+2))
    valueArgs = append(valueArgs, u.Name, u.Email)
}
query := fmt.Sprintf("INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES %s", strings.Join(valueStrings, ","))
_, err := db.ExecContext(ctx, query, valueArgs...)

Description: Use a nullable deleted_at timestamp column to mark records as deleted without physically removing them.
Do: Add a deleted_at column and filter it in queries; GORM supports this natively.
Dont: Do not hard-delete records when you need audit trails or the ability to restore data.

Description: Define a repository interface so business logic does not depend on a specific database implementation.
Do: Define an interface and implement it with a concrete struct that holds the DB handle.
Dont: Do not pass *sql.DB or *gorm.DB directly into business logic; it couples layers tightly.

Description: Use testcontainers-go to spin up a real database in Docker for integration tests.
Do: Use testcontainers to create a disposable database container per test suite.
Dont: Do not mock the entire database layer for integration tests; it hides real query bugs.
Code Good:
func TestUserRepo(t *testing.T) {
    ctx := context.Background()
    container, err := postgres.Run(ctx, "postgres:16-alpine",
        postgres.WithDatabase("testdb"),
        postgres.WithUsername("test"),
        postgres.WithPassword("test"),
        testcontainers.WithWaitStrategy(
            wait.ForLog("database system is ready to accept connections").
                WithOccurrence(2).WithStartupTimeout(30*time.Second)),
    )
    testcontainers.CleanupContainer(t, container)
    require.NoError(t, err)
    connStr, err := container.ConnectionString(ctx, "sslmode=disable")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    db, err := sql.Open("postgres", connStr)
    require.NoError(t, err)
    // Run tests against real DB
}

Description: Build connection strings from environment variables with explicit parameter names instead of hardcoding.
Do: Load DSN components from environment variables and set sslmode explicitly.
Dont: Do not hardcode credentials in source code or use sslmode=disable in production.
